coredata.dc:title: The investigation of structural alteration of raw materials used to attain graphene quantum dots in different prolysis conditions
coredata.dc:creator: Kir, Şifa
Dehri, İlyas
Önal, Yunus
Esen, Ramazan
Başar, Canan Akmil
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots which is one of the carbon allotrope become widespread an industrial usage due to the unique optical and electrical properties. Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) is obtained from the materials such as graphite, glycine, alginate, glucose whose structure contains carbon atoms. In this study, molasses that are used to attain GQDs are carbonized at the temperature of 200, 250, 300, 350 and 400 °C for 2 h. The DTA method was used to determine the melting temperature of the molasses. In order to determine the elemental and optical properties of the obtained graphene quantum dots particles, XRD, SEM, Elemental Analysis, Fluorescence Spectrometry techniques and UV-Visible Spectroscopy are used. Mass loss values was also determined and studied its effects were the outcome. The results obtained from these techniques show that the material obtained from the molasses is GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Molasses
Carbonization

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Graph of temperature (°C) loss of mass% of molasses.
FT-IR spectra of molasses and products; M-0 (a), M-200 (b), M-250 (c), M-300 (d), M-350 (e), M-400 (f).
XRD spectra of products; M-250 (a), M-300 (b), M-350 (c), M-400 (d).

UV–VIS absorption spectra of M-0-SFP (a), M-0-MF (b) solutions (A); from left to right; (pure water, M-0-SFP and M-0-MF solutions) photos under the visible region (a), the UV-C lamp (b), the UV-A lamp (c) (B); emission (C) and excitation (D) spectra of the M-0-SFP solution; emission (E) and excitation (F) spectra of the M-0-MF solution.

UV–VIS absorption spectra of M-200-SFP (a), M-200-MF (b) solutions (A); from left to right; (pure water, M-200-SFP and M-200-MF solutions) photos under the visible region (a), the UV-C lamp (b), the UV-A lamp (c) (B); emission and excitation spectra of the M-200-SFP solution (C); emission and excitation spectra of the M-200-MF solution (D).

UV–VIS absorption spectra of M-250-SFP (a), M-250-MF (b) solutions (A); from left to right; (pure water, M-250-SFP and M-250-MF solutions) photos under the visible region (a), the UV-C lamp (b), the UV-A lamp (c) (B); emission and excitation spectra of the M-250-SFP solution (C); emission and excitation spectra of the M-250-MF solution (D).

UV–VIS absorption spectra of M-300-SFP (a), M-300-MF (b) solutions (A); from left to right; (pure water, M-300-SFP and M-300-MF solutions) photos under the visible region (a), the UV-C lamp (b), the UV-A lamp (c) (B); emission and excitation spectra of the M-300-SFP solution (C); emission and excitation spectra of the M-300-MF solution (D).

UV–VIS absorption spectra of M-350-SFP (a), M-350-MF (b) solutions (A); from left to right; (pure water, M-350-SFP and M-350-MF solutions) photos under the visible region (a), the UV-C lamp (b), the UV-A lamp (c) (B); emission and excitation spectra of the M-350-SFP solution (C); emission and excitation spectra of the M-350-MF solution (D).

UV–VIS absorption spectra of M-400-SFP (a), M-400-MF (b) solutions (A); from left to right; (pure water, M-400-SFP and M-400-MF solutions) photos under the visible region (a), the UV-C lamp (b), the UV-A lamp (c) (B); emission and excitation spectra of the M-400-SFP solution (C); emission and excitation spectra of the M-400-MF solution (D).
The codes of the products and the molasses.
The codes of solutions of the products and the molasses.
Size calculation of molasses and products showing sharp excitation and emission.

head.ce:title: The investigation of structural alteration of raw materials used to attain graphene quantum dots in different prolysis conditions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Science and Letters, Department of Chemistry, Çukurova University, 01330, Adana, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Science and Letters, Department of Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Engineering, Department of Chemical Engineering, İnönü University, 44280, Malatya, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Engineering, Department of Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Science and Letters, Department of Physics, Çukurova University 01330, Adana, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Science and Letters, Department of Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cFaculty of Science and Letters, Department of Physics, Çukurova University 01330, Adana, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots which is one of the carbon allotrope become widespread an industrial usage due to the unique optical and electrical properties. Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) is obtained from the materials such as graphite, glycine, alginate, glucose whose structure contains carbon atoms. In this study, molasses that are used to attain GQDs are carbonized at the temperature of 200, 250, 300, 350 and 400 °C for 2 h. The DTA method was used to determine the melting temperature of the molasses. In order to determine the elemental and optical properties of the obtained graphene quantum dots particles, XRD, SEM, Elemental Analysis, Fluorescence Spectrometry techniques and UV-Visible Spectroscopy are used. Mass loss values was also determined and studied its effects were the outcome. The results obtained from these techniques show that the material obtained from the molasses is GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: A new form of carbon is discovered every day, as a result of thousands of years of research on the carbon element, which is the basis of life [1,2]. Each discovery results in the expansion of the carbon family [3–5]. Carbon nanoscale materials with new extraordinary properties discovered find application site in many areas. Every new discovery allows mankind to live more comfortably. Graphene [6,7], carbon nanotube [8,9], nanodiamonds [10,11], fulleren [12,13], carbon dots (CDs) [14,15], graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [16,17], carbon nanohorns [18,19], carbon nano-onion [20,21], graphene oxide (GO) [22,23], carbon nanofiber [24,25] are parts of the family of carbon nanomaterials.

Graphene quantum dots, from the carbon nanomaterial family, attracts the attention of researchers with its unique properties and studies on GQDs and heteroatom doping GQDs are rapidly increasing [26–32]. The fluorescent carbon nanomaterial graphene quantum dots was produced by Ponomarenko and Geim in 2008 based on the 2004 work on carbon dots by Xu et al. [33]. Generally, it consists of less than five layers of graphene. The planar dimensions and height of graphene sheets in GQDs are 1.5 to 100 nm and 0.5 to 5 nm, respectively. Their shapes are mostly circular and elliptical, but there are quadrilaterals and hexagonal points. Basically, the properties of GQDs are similar to graphene and consist of C, O, H and carbonyl, carboxyl, hydroxyl and epoxy as surface groups and crystal structure [34]. GQDs can be produced from citric acid [35] by carbonization method, graphite by Hummers method [36,37], graphene oxide by chemical cutting method [38], graphite rod by electrochemical exfoliation method [39], glycosis by pyrolysis method [40], graphite broken down by amidative cutting method [41]. In addition, it is produced by alkaline-catalyzed water-phase molecular fusion method from pyrene [42], a facile microwave-assisted hydrothermal method from glucose derivatives [43], graphene oxide and dimethylformamide from solvothermal method [44], and raw plant leaves from hydrothermal method ((Neem (Azadirachta indica) and Fenugreek (Trigonella foenum-graecum)) [45].

Thanks to its excellent optical, electrical and optoelectronic properties, graphene quantum dots are used in the fields of catalysis, drug delivery, bio-imaging, photodynamic therapy, optical detection, electrochemical sensors, optoelectronics, display, optoelectric detectors, solar cells, light emitting diodes, fluorescent matter, photocatalysis, supercapacitor and lithium ion batteries [33,46-50].

Molasses obtained as a by-product during sugar beet production in sugar factories is a matter with dark coffee colored and molasses consistency [51,52]. According to the literature, it is calculated that 7 tons of sugar beet is required to obtain 1 ton of sugar and between 0.25 and 0.35 tons of molasses. Molasses is basically used as a fermentation raw material for the production of baker's yeast, for the production of amino acids, antibiotics or citric acids in the chemical or pharmaceutical industry, and as animal food [53].

In this study, GQDs has been synthesized from sugar beet molasses, which is also a biomass, by carbonization method, which is a green synthesis method and was characterized by various methods. GQDs are carbonized at the temperature of 200, 250, 300, 350 and 400 °C for 2 h. The effect of the pore size of the filtration apparatus on the optical properties of GQDs has been investigated. The DTA technique was utilized to find the melting temperature of the molasses. The elemental and optical properties of the obtained graphene quantum dots particles were studied by elemental analysis, fluorescence spectrometry techniques and UV–visible spectroscopy. XRD and SEM analysis were used to determined crystal properties and morphology. The results obtained from these methods demonstrated that the material obtained from the molasses is GQDs.

Molasses, which is a by-product of processing sugar beet, was used as a raw material for the synthesis of graphene quantum dot [54]. Molasses obtained from Malatya Sugar Factory which is exposed as a by-product in process as a result of processing sugar beet and used in bioethanol production.

Degradation temperatures of molasses in the nitrogen atmosphere in the temperature range of 25–1100 °C were determined with the help of DTA (Mettler Toledo Brand TGA / DSC 3+). Molasses (5 g) is exposed as a by-product in process as a result of processing sugar beet was taken and put in petri dishes. It was then subjected to carbonization in the air environment at ash furnace (Protherm) for 2 h at temperatures of 200; 250; 300; 350 and 400 °C to obtain GQDs. The samples obtained as a result of the carbonization process and molasses were placed in plastic sample containers for characterization experiments. The codes of the products and molasses which have made for this work are given in Table 1 .

Molasses and carbonized molasses samples were taken 0.1 g from each; 100 mL water were added over these samples. Two solutions were prepared from each. One of the solutions prepared in the same proportions of all samples was filtered with a simple filter paper (2–4 µm) and the other with a membrane filter of 0.22 µm. Photos of the solutions were taken in the visible area. Camag UV cabin is used to observe the colors of fluorescence under UV-A and UV-C lamp.

UV/VIS (Agilent Cary 100) and photoluminescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies Cary Eclipse) were used to determine the maximum excitation and emission values of the material and the excitation-related emission activities. The codes of prepared solutions are given in Table 2 .

According to molasses TGA-DTA graph (Fig. 1 ), thermal degradation is observed to be around 140 °C. When the TGA-DTA results of the raw sample are examined; three different mass loss curves are seen in the TGA curve.

This indicates that there are different molecular groups in the raw material. In the DTA curve, the endothermic peak of the removal of moisture from the structure is seen quite flat. Within the wide and flat endothermic peak in region 90–250 °C, a separate endothermic peak around 145 °C shows that low molecular weight organic component(s) other than moisture are present in the structure. The organic structures that decompose in the raw material in a temperature range of about 200–700 °C are exothermic, quite flat and wide peak is observed. Consistent with the TGA results, it shows that different molecular groups are present in the structure.

Based on the results of DTA, 5 g molasses were carbonized at different temperatures for 2 h in ash furnace and the change of% mass loss with temperature as a result of this process is given in Fig. 2 . When Fig. 2 was examined, it was observed that the effect of temperature after 300 °C on mass loss was not significant.

Elemental analysis of products are given in Table 3 . When the results of the elemental analysis (Thermo Scientific Flash 2000) of the products were examined, no significant changes were observed in the C% ratio due to the increase in temperature.

Overall, no significant change was observed in the values of%H,%N,%S and%O. The decrease in the amount of%N and%H can be explained by the removal of NH3 gas from the main structure when the structure of molasses is considered to be amide.

The FT-IR (Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS10) graph of the molasses (M-0) and the products obtained from molasses (M-200, M-250, M-300, M-350, M-400) by carbonization with 2 h at temperatures 200; 250; 300; 350 and 400 °C is given in Fig. 3 . It was observed from Fig. 3 that the functional groups of the product (M-200) obtained as a result of carbonization process with temperature of 200 °C and 2 h were the same as those of molasses (M-0). In all samples, cellulosic hydroxyl group (-OH) and NH group are present in the 3400 cm−1 band, and the amount of cellulosic hydroxyl group and NH group is decreased with the separation of H2O and NH3 from the structure due to increased temperature in heat treatment.

Since the sulfur content of all samples was low, no significant peak was observed in the FT-IR spectrum. It can be concluded that the FTIR peaks of organic nitrogen compounds can be clearly seen in the 1560–1540, 1380–1350, 1640–1485, 1355–1320 cm−1 region [55]. All samples have an aliphatic CH bond at 2900 cm−1. All samples have NH bonds in band 1580–1650 cm−1; C = C bonds in band 1650–1566 cm−1; and CH bonds in band 1450–1375 cm−1. In M-0 and M-200 samples, the CN and CO bond in the 1250–1020 cm−1 band; the CO-C bond in the 1050–1040 cm−1 band; the C = C bond in the 990 cm−1 and 930 cm−1 band moved away from the structure due to heat treatment.

XRD analysis (Rigaku Miniflex II) is given in Fig. 4 . XRD analysis results of the samples obtained as a result of carbonization of molasses with 2 h at temperatures of 250, 300, 350 and 400 °C are examined; it is observed that these samples have crystal structure.

M-250, M-300, M-350 and M-400 samples have sharp peaks of layer separation of 3.11; 3.12; 3.12; 3.12 Å and at 2θ=28.67; 28.57; 28.59; 28.59°, respectively. M-300 substance with peak height of 1149 cps has the sharpest pike. The peak seen at 2θ=28° can be attributed to the graphene/graphite phase. The peak which is observed at 2θ=40.78° can be attributed to the cellulosic structure. Again the M-250, M-300, M-350 and M-400 samples have sharp peak (2.21 Å) with the same layer separation at 2θ=40.78; 40.71; 40.75 and 40.72° respectively.M-350 and M-400 samples shows a second peak around 30° that can be attributed GQDs variations with a m-GQDs (molasses-GQDs) layer separations of 2.99 Å. X-ray diffraction data summary is given in Table 4 . It shows that there is better structural order with the increase of temperature. It is clear that as the temperature rises, new component(s) will be formed. And the material will go structural arrangement depending on the environmental conditions (inert and/or air and/or oxygen) in which the organic compounds decompose [56].

Because the molasses and the sample obtained as a result of carbonization of molasses with a temperature of 200 °C for a period of 2 h was viscous, SEM analysis (Fei Quanta 650 Model) of these samples could not be done. The results of SEM analysis of other samples are explained. SEM image of M-250 is seen in Fig. 5 . When the SEM image of the substance (M-250) obtained from the carbonization of molasses at 250 °C for 2 h is examined, it is seen that the small molecules are removed from the structure by the effect of heat treatment and that the hollows resemble the bat wing and some areas layers are formed.

In some areas; there are hillocks with a diameter of 17.12; 24.32; 32.19; 50.52; 74.87; 101.8; 164.3 nm (Fig. 5). SEM image of M-300 is given in Fig. 6 . When the SEM image of the substance (M-300) obtained as a result of the carbonization of molasses at 300 °C for 2 h is examined, it is seen that the images resembling honey comb are formed by the effect of heat treatment. The inside of the honeycomb appears to be empty and has nano walls with 22.48; 48.43 nm thick. The distance from one nano wall to the other nano wall is 311.7; 380.1; 451.6 and 476.7 nm. Layers have formed in some areas. In some areas, nano-hillocks similar to those of M-250 matter are observed to form (Fig. 6).

These hillocks have a diameter greater than the M-250 sample with diameter lengths of 87.31; 120.7; 237.4; 291.6; 348.0 and 475.8 nm. In some areas, pits were formed (Fig. 6). When the SEM image of the substance (M-350) obtained as a result of the carbonization of molasses at 350 °C for 2 h is examined, it is seen that the small molecules are separated from the structure by the effect of heat treatment and the main structure is revealed. The main structure consists of 22.73; 38.24; 50.27; 79.20 and 116.10 nm in diameter. Due to the effect of heat treatment, some areas are porous and some areas are layered structures (Fig. 7 ).

As molecules are separated from the structure by the effect of heat, the presence of indented protruding structures has been identified (Fig. 7). When the SEM image of the sample (M-400) obtained from the carbonization of molasses at a temperature of 400 °C for 2 h is examined, it is seen that the SEM photos show similarities with the sample M-350, as in the XRD and FT-IR analysis results. Again, with the effect of heat treatment, the main structure is revealed by the separation of small molecules from the structure. It stands out in the layered structures in the image. In some areas, it is seen that cavities are formed, and there are molecules of different shapes and sizes in this cavity (Fig. 8 ).

Photos under UV-A; UV-C lamps, and the results of UV/VIS; photoluminescence analysis of the aqueous solutions of molasses and products are explained. When the UV–VIS analysis results of both molasses solutions prepared under the same conditions, it was observed that the different filtration apparatus does not change the absorbance of the solution. This property can be explained as the optical absorption and photolumiscence (PL) emission properties given by particles (quantum dots) with sizes very smaller than 0.22 µm (filter size). Some of the materials are dissolved in pure water. So, varying filter size does not affect UV absorption and emission data. The M-0-SFP solution yielded peaks at 259 and 197 nm. The M-0-MF solution yielded peaks at 260 and 200 nm. n-σ* and π-π* electron transition occurs in this UV region in both solutions as seen in Fig. 9. A.

0.1 g molasses solution prepared by taking 100 mL pure water as a solvent gives light yellow color in the visible region, the blue colored fluorescence has been observed under UV-C and UV-A lamp. There is no visible difference in terms of color and intensity of the fluorescence data of solutions filtered with a simple filter paper or a 0.22 µm membrane filter (Fig. 9.B). The sharp peak at 468.03 nm in the emission graph obtained by excitation at 234.6 nm maximum; the sharp peak at 242 nm in the excitation graph obtained by emission at 486.06 nm maximum shows the presence of quantum dot in M-0-SFP solution (Fig. 9.C-D). The sharp peak at 560 nm in the emission graph obtained by excitation at 279.06 nm; the sharp peak at 299.07 nm in the excitation graph obtained by emission at 597 nm shows the presence of quantum dot in the M-0-MF solution (Fig. 9.E-F). When the both solutions compared, it has been observed that the fluorescence intensity decreases due to the decrease in the number of nanoscale particles passing through the solution as a result of the change of the filtration apparatus.

When the UV–VIS analysis of both solutions of the material obtained as a result of the carbonization of molasses at 200 °C for 2 h, it was observed that the absorption intensity decreased even if the pore size of the filtration apparatus decreased. n-σ* and π-π* electron transition occurs in this UV region in both solutions as seen in Fig. 10. A. It has been observed that the solutions give light brown color in the visible region and show blue colored fluorescence under UV-C and UV-A lamps. There is no visible difference in terms of color and intensity of the fluorescence data of solutions filtered with a simple filter paper or a 0.22 µm membrane filter (Fig. 10.B).

The sharp peak at 725.07 nm in the emission graph obtained from the 363.07 nm excitation of the filtered solution by simple strainer paper shows the presence of quantum dots in the solution (Fig. 10.C). The emission graph obtained by excitation of the filtered solution with 0.22 µm membrane filter at 366 nm, excitation graph obtained by emission at 450 nm is also given in Fig. 10.D. When larger particles pass through the filter, the number of GQDs in the solution increases and this results higher fluorescence.

When the emission graphs of both solutions are compared, the sharp peak seen in Fig. 10.C. It has not been observed in Fig. 10.D., because of the passage of smaller sized molecules into the solution. When larger particles pass, the GQDs numbers in the mosaic texture within them increase and affect the fluorescence. It was observed that absorption intensity did not change but peak maximums changed when the pore size of the filtration apparatus was reduced when UV–VIS analysis was examined of both solutions of the material obtained as a result of the carbonization of molasses at 250 °C for 2 h. n-σ* and π-π* electron transition occurs in this UV region in both solutions as seen in Fig. 11. A.

It has been observed that solutions give brown color in the visible region and show blue colored fluorescence under UV-C and UV-A lamps. There is no visible difference in terms of color and intensity of the fluorescence data of solutions filtered with a simple filter paper or a 0.22 µm membrane filter (Fig. 11.B). The emission graph obtained by excitation at 364 nm and the excitation graph obtained by emission at 458.93 nm of the filtered solution with simple strainer paper is also given in Fig. 11.C. The emission graph obtained by excitation at 396 nm maximum and excitation graph obtained by emission at 485 nm maximum of the filtered solution with a 0.22 µm membrane filter is given in Fig. 11.D. When the emission graphs of both solutions are compared, sharp peak in Fig. 11.C at 725.07 nm has not been observed in Fig. 11.D due to the passage of smaller molecules into the solution.

When the result of UV–VIS analysis of both solutions of the material obtained from the carbonization of molasses at a temperature of 300 °C for 2 h, it was observed that the filtration apparatus did not change the absorption intensity when the pore size decreased, but changed peak maximums. n-σ* and π-π* excitations are observed in both solutions (Fig. 12. A). It has been observed that solutions give a dark brown color in the visible region and show blue colored fluorescence under UV-C and UV-A lamps. There is no visible difference in terms of color and intensity of the fluorescence data of solutions filtered with a simple filter paper or a 0.22 µm membrane filter (Fig. 12.B). The emission graph obtained by excitation at 374 nm maximum and excitation graph obtained by emission at 471 nm maximum of the filtered solution with simple strainer paper is also given in Fig. 12.C. The emission graph obtained by excitation at 448.03 nm and excitation graph obtained by emission at 524.02 nm of the filtered solution with 0.22 µm membrane filter is also given in Fig. 12.D. When the emission graphs of both solutions are compared, the solution filtered by simple strainer paper shows a low-intensity sharp pick up around 750 nm, but this peak is not seen in the other solution. Fluorescence intensity also decreases due to the passing of smaller sized molecules.

When the result of UV–VIS analysis of both solutions obtained as a result of the carbonization of molasses at 350 °C for 2 h were examined it was observed that the absorption intensity changed the very little when the pore size of the filtration decreased. n-σ* excitations are observed in both solutions (Fig. 13. A). It has been observed that solutions give light brown color in the visible region and show blue colored fluorescence under UV-C and UV-A lamps. There is no visible difference in terms of color and intensity of the fluorescence data of solutions filtered with a simple filter paper or a 0.22 µm membrane filter (Fig. 13.B).

The emission graph obtained by excitation at 354 nm and excitation graph obtained by emission at 440 nm of the filtered solution with simple strainer paper is also given in Fig. 13.C. The emission graph obtained by excitation at 327.96 nm and excitation graph obtained by emission at 418.93 nm of the filtered solution with 0.22 µm membrane filter is also given in Fig. 13.D. When the emission graphs of both solutions are compared, a sharp peak is observed at 707.07 nm in the solution filtered by simple strainer paper; a lower sharp peak is observed at 654 nm in the other solution.

When the UV–VIS analysis of both solutions of the material obtained from the carbonization of molasses at 400 °C for 2 h were evalated, it was observed that changed the absorption intensity very little when the pore size of the filtration was reduced (Fig. 14. A). It has been observed that the solutions give light yellow color in the visible region and show blue colored fluorescence under UV-C and UV-A lamps. The carbon particles suspended in the solution filtered by simple strainer paper are noticeable to the eye. This is also noticed under the UV-A lamp. The solution filtered by a 0.22 µm membrane filter gives a brighter blue color; the solution filtered by a simple filter paper gives a matte blue color (Fig. 14.B).

The emission graph obtained by excitation at 386 nm and excitation graph obtained by emission at 466 nm of the filtered solution with simple strainer paper is given in Fig. 14.C. The emission graph obtained by excitation at 250 nm and excitation graph obtained by emission at 418.93 nm of the filtered solution with 0.22 µm membrane filter is also given in Fig. 14.D. When the emission graphs of both solutions are compared, the solution filtered by simple strainer paper shows a sharp peak at 770.95 nm and the other solution at 497.01 nm. It was observed that the fluorescence intensity of the solution filtered by a membrane filter of 0.22 µm was greater than that of the solution filtered by simple filter paper. It was observed that the fluorescence intensity of the M-400-MF solutions was higher compared to M-400-SFP solutions, but the sharpness decreased.

Table 5 summarizes the sharp excitation peaks obtained at this work. The sharp excitation peaks in general did not give sharp emission. This property can be explained as size distribution of particles in the water environment is sharp but the particles closely thermalizes the absorbed light since they are closely intact of the water molecules. The samples show sharp excitations. *Size calculated similar to reference [57].

When XRD results of M-250, M-300, M-350 and M-400 samples are examined, it is seen that all the samples are crystal structure. When the results of the SEM analysis are examined, nano-sized hillocks are noted in all the samples. This group of samples shows sharp excitation peaks but this is not seen in the emission. We propose that particles very well intact with the environment which helps to thermalize the absorbed light. SEM studies shows that there are different regions in the solid samples, ranging carbon nanodots to honey combs as seen in Fig. 6. The filtering with 0.22 µm membran filter instead of simple filter paper reduces the PL data, we propose that this effect is due to dissolving of the material continues this reduces the PL properties. The best PL data is obtained with M-400, after 400 °C carbonization the samples looses much of the constituents.

Şifa Kir: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. İlyas Dehri: Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision. Yunus Önal: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision. Ramazan Esen: Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Canan Akmil Başar: Investigation, Writing – original draft, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Çukurova University Scientific Research Unit (Project No: FDK-2016–6191).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: C60 Topological Isomers: other Carbon Allotropes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.C. Dobrowolski, A.P. Mazurek, C60 Topological Isomers: other Carbon Allotropes, J. Phys. Chem. A.102 (1998) 5260–5262. https://doi.org/10.1021/jp980277
M. Hu, F. Tian, Z. Zhao and et al., Exotic cubic carbon allotropes, J. Phys. Chem. C.116 (2012) 24233–24238. https://doi.org/10.1021/jp3064323

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novamene: a new class of carbon allotropes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.A. Burchfield, M.A. Fahim, R.S. Wittman and et al., Novamene: a new class of carbon allotropes, Heliyon. 3 (2017) e00242https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2017.e00242
F. Delodovici, N. Manini, R.S. Wittman and et al., Protomene: a new carbon allotrope, Carbon N Y. 126 (2018) 574–579. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2017.10.069

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A new super hard carbon allotrope: orthorhombic C20

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Wei, C. Zhao, M. Zhang and et al., A new super hard carbon allotrope: orthorhombic C20, Phys. Lett. A. 382 (2018) 1685–1689. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.physleta.2018.04.024
